---
title: "Introduction"
subtitle: "Dynamic Reports and Data Management"
day: "Day 1"
---

## Before we start {background-color="#8D203D"}

::: {style="text-align: center; margin-top: 1em; color: white;"}
**Think of a time you had to update a results section (e.g., after review).**
:::

. . .

::: {style="text-align: center; color: white;"}
*How long did it take?*
:::

. . .

::: {style="text-align: center; color: white;"}
*Could you find where your original analyses had come from?*
:::

. . .

::: {style="text-align: center; color: white;"}
*How confident were you nothing broke?*
:::

. . .

::: {style="text-align: center; color: white;"}
*Could a colleague reproduce it from scratch?*
:::

## Learning objectives

By the end of this session you will be able to:

::: incremental
- Set up a self-contained, reproducible RProject
- Explain what literate programming is and why it matters
- Use inline R code to report results dynamically
- Save and load models as `.rds` objects
- Apply consistent naming conventions to files and objects
:::

## How this workshop works {data-stack-name="Workshop overview"}

::: incremental
- **Mix of short lectures and hands-on exercises**
- Follow along in your own RProject
- Red slides = time to pause, discuss, or try something
- Ask questions any time — there are no silly ones
:::

. . .

::: callout-tip
## Running dataset
We'll use a real dataset throughout -- a clean, well-documented dataset so we can focus on the *workflow*, not the data.
:::

### What we assume

::: incremental
- You have used R before
- You are comfortable with basic `tidyverse` (`filter()`, `mutate()`, `ggplot()`)
- You have R, RStudio/Positron, and Quarto installed
- You do **not** need to be an expert, that's why we're here
:::

. . . 

::: callout-note
If you haven't installed the required packages yet, now is a good time.  
See the [Preface](../index.html) for the full list.
:::

### Why reproducibility matters

::: incremental
- Analyses change; reviewers ask for revisions, data gets updated
- Copy-paste errors between R output and manuscript are common
- A reproducible workflow means **one source of truth**
:::


### 💬 Turn & talk {background-color="#8D203D"}

::: {style="text-align: center; margin-top: 2em; color: white;"}

Discuss with your neighbour:

*What does your current project structure look like?*  
*How do you get your analyses to your manuscript results section?*
:::

## The problem with static workflows {data-stack-name="Dynamic reports"}
```r
# Run model → copy number → paste into Word → reviewer asks to rerun → ...
```

::: incremental
- Output lives in your head (or in a Word comment)
- Figures saved manually, versioned as `fig_final_v3_ACTUAL_FINAL.png`
  + you don't even know where the code for that figure is...
- Collaborators can't reproduce your results
:::

## The solution: dynamic reports

::: incremental
- Code, text, and output live in **one document**
- Re-render = everything updates automatically
- Works for HTML, PDF, LaTeX, and Word output (among others)
:::

## Some basics {data-stack-name="Basics"}

### Literate programming

::: callout-note
## What is literate programming?
Code and prose are written **together** in the same document; the analysis tells its own story.
:::

. . .

- Introduced by Donald Knuth (1984)
- R Markdown and Quarto are modern implementations
- The document *is* the analysis, not a write-up of it

. . .
```markdown
We fitted a linear model predicting body mass from species and sex
(`r nrow(df_clean)` observations). The effect of sex was
`r report_t(m1, "sexMale")`.
```

### Naming conventions

::: callout-tip
## Files
Use lowercase, no spaces, separate with `_`:
`01_clean_data.R`, `02_fit_models.R`, `fig_mass_by_species.png`
:::

. . .

::: callout-tip
## Objects
Be descriptive and consistent:
`df_raw`, `df_clean`, `m1_null`, `m2_species`, `fig_mass`
:::

. . .

::: callout-warning
## Avoid
`final`, `v2`, `NEW`, `test`, spaces in filenames, `data2`
:::

### Modular analyses

For larger projects, split your analysis into separate scripts rather than one long `.qmd`:
```
R/
├── 01_load_data.R
├── 02_clean_data.R
├── 03_fit_models.R
└── 04_make_figures.R
```

### Source scripts

Source them in your `.qmd` render script:
```r
#| output: false
source(here::here("R", "01_load_data.R"))
source(here::here("R", "02_clean_data.R"))
```

### Load in objects

As a rule of thumb, each script should have a single output: a preprocessed/clean/tidy dataset, figures or tables, models, etc.

My typical scripts for a project: 

- `00-setup.qmd`: anything needed to set the project up (e.g., installing packages)
- `01-processing.qmd`: reading in raw data (from `data/raw/`) --> pre-processing / cleaning --> saving cleaned data (to `data/tidy/`)
- `02-eda.qmd`: Exploratory Data Analysis --> demographic info --> visualising distributions --> tables and figures to understand the data
- `03-analyses.qmd`: fitting models --> sanity checks for exclusion criteria --> variable transformations (e.g., centring predictors, setting contrast coding) --> fitting models --> checking assumptions --> saving models (to `output/models/`) --> inspecting results
- `04-figures.qmd`: producing publication figures (usually a lot of options, indicating which ones get chosen for the manuscript) --> saving figures for manuscript (to `output/figures/`)
- `05-report.qmd`: reporting the findings
- `06-tables.qmd`: tables for reporting (sometimes not its own script; saved to `output/tables/`)
. . .

::: callout-tip
## Why modular?
- Each script has one job
- Easy to re-run just one step
- Easier to debug and hand over
:::

## Tools we'll use

| Tool | Purpose |
|---|---|
| RProject | Self-contained, portable project |
| `renv` | Reproducible package versions |
| Quarto | Dynamic documents and reports |
| `here` | Reliable file paths |
| `tidyverse` | Data wrangling |
| `gtsummary`, `modelsummary` | Publication tables |
| `ggplot2` | Publication figures |

## Workshop structure

**Session 1**

- Project setup, folder structure, `renv`
- Data wrangling recap
- Summary statistics and demographics tables
- Publication figures

. . .

**Session 2**

- Reporting model results
- Summary tables with `gt` and `flextable`
- Quarto for manuscripts
- Exporting to LaTeX and Word

## By the end of this workshop...

::: incremental
- Your analysis lives in a single reproducible `.qmd` file
- Tables and figures update automatically when data changes
- You can export to Word for co-authors or LaTeX for journals
- Your project is self-contained and shareable
:::
